steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence (quantitative) question. The passage claims peaking plants 'can generate more megawatt-hours of power per year than some run-of-river plants.' The blank must complete the example ('For example, the ______') by using data from the table to directly support that specific claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘average power generated annually by the Great Falls plant is higher than that generated by any of the run-of-river plants in the table.’: Great Falls (peaking) generates 124,392 MWh/yr, which is higher than both Scanlon (7,511 MWh/yr) and Kansas River (15,345 MWh/yr). This directly proves that a peaking plant can out-generate run-of-river plants on an annual basis. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Scanlon plant, which is a run-of-river plant, has more generators than any of the other plants in the table.’: The table contains no data about number of generators. This claim cannot be supported by the table at all. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘run-of-river plant with the highest average annual power generation in the table generates more electricity than the peaking plant with the highest annual power generation in the table.’: This directly contradicts the claim — Kansas River (run-of-river, 15,345 MWh/yr) does not generate more than Great Falls (peaking, 124,392 MWh/yr). ✗
  • Choice D — ‘average power generated annually by the Kansas River plant is higher than that generated by the Scanlon plant.’: Both are run-of-river plants. Comparing two run-of-river plants says nothing about whether a peaking plant can exceed a run-of-river plant. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
